summary:
The extinction of Pleistocene megafauna in the Americas — large mammals including woolly mammoths, mastodons, American horses, camels, giant ground sloths, short-faced bears, saber-toothed cats, and many others — was substantially completed between approximately 13,000 and 10,000 BCE, coinciding with the arrival and spread of human hunters and the climatic upheavals of the late glacial period including the [[event:1261|Younger Dryas]].

North America lost approximately 70–75% of its large mammal genera during this interval, a loss of ecological diversity from which the continent never fully recovered. South America experienced a similarly catastrophic megafaunal collapse. The extinctions in the Americas stand out from those in Africa and tropical Asia, where large mammals largely survived — a contrast often attributed to the fact that African and Asian megafauna co-evolved alongside human hunters over millions of years, developing behavioral responses to predation, whereas American megafauna had no prior exposure to human hunters and were therefore "naive prey." The [[event:1283|Clovis culture]] and its contemporaries are associated with hunting mammoth, mastodon, and other large prey.

The relative importance of human hunting versus climate change in driving the American extinctions remains one of the most debated questions in Quaternary science. The "overkill hypothesis" — arguing that human hunting alone was sufficient to exterminate the megafauna — is supported by some models but questioned by evidence that climate-driven habitat loss severely stressed megafaunal populations before and alongside human arrival. Current consensus tends toward a synergistic model in which both factors contributed, with human hunting delivering the final blow to populations already stressed by the collapse of Pleistocene ecosystems.

The loss of megafauna had cascading ecological consequences: the disappearance of large herbivores restructured plant communities, eliminated seed-dispersal services, altered landscape hydrology, and removed prey species that had supported large carnivores. These changes fundamentally shaped the ecology of the Americas as humans came to occupy them, determining the biological landscape in which all subsequent indigenous American civilizations developed.
